site.title: The Real GDP
site.description: Measuring real wealth beyond GDP. 5 dimensions, open data.
site.tagline: What if we measured wealth the right way?

home.hero_sub: GDP tells you how much money moves. Not how well a country is doing. We measure the 5 things that actually make a country wealthy.
home.dimensions_sub: Every country gets a score from 0 to 100 in each dimension. The Real GDP is the average.
home.ranking_sub: The bar shows the 5 dimensions stacked. Longer bar = higher Real GDP. The grey bar is traditional GDP per capita for comparison.

explain.title: What is the Real GDP?
explain.sub: A simple explanation. No economics degree required.
explain.s1_p1: GDP measures how much money moves through a country in a year. That's it. If a country cuts down all its forests and sells the wood, GDP goes up. If a hurricane destroys a city and people rebuild, GDP goes up. If everyone gets sick and spends money on hospitals, GDP goes up.
explain.s1_p2: GDP is like measuring your health by how much money you spend at the pharmacy. Spending more doesn't mean you're healthier — it might mean the opposite.
explain.s2_p1: Think of a country like a person. A wealthy person doesn't just have cash in their pocket. They have:
explain.s2_physical: A house, a car, tools — things they've built or bought that help them work and live
explain.s2_human: Education, health, skills — what they know and how long they'll live
explain.s2_natural: Land, clean water, a garden — natural resources they can use
explain.s2_institutions: Good contracts, fair courts, trustworthy neighbours — rules that make life predictable
explain.s2_technology: Know-how, inventions, clever solutions — the ability to do more with less
explain.s3_p1: We take public data from the World Bank, the United Nations, and other reliable sources. For each of the 5 dimensions, we create a score from 0 (worst) to 100 (best). Then we average them.
explain.s3_p2: That's the Real GDP. Simple. No black box, no secret formula. You can download the data and check it yourself.
explain.s4_p1: Because it changes the story. By GDP alone, the United States is the richest country in the world. But when you look at all 5 dimensions — education, health, environment, institutions, innovation — the picture is very different.
explain.s4_p2: Small countries like Norway, Switzerland, and Denmark score higher because they invest in people, protect their environment, and build institutions that work.
explain.s4_p3: A country that gets rich by destroying its forests, ignoring its people's health, or letting corruption run wild isn't really wealthy. It's cashing in its future.
explain.s5_p1: No. Each dimension compresses messy reality into a single number. The weights are equal — that's a choice, not a law of physics. Italy's 'Institutions: 58' hides huge differences between Milan and Reggio Calabria.
explain.s5_p2: But it's better than pretending GDP tells the whole story. And we publish all the data so you can build a better version.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.sub: How we calculate the Real GDP score
methodology.intro: The Real GDP is the simple average of 5 normalized dimensions, each scored 0-100.
methodology.limitations_text: This is an approximation. Each dimension compresses complex realities into a single number. The weights are equal by default — a political choice. We publish the data so you can weight them yourself.

country.vs_gdp: Real GDP vs GDP per capita

footer.built: Built with open data from World Bank, UNDP, WIPO
